1: Amaziah became king at 25 and reigned 29 years in Jerusalem. His mother's name was Jehoaddan of Jerusalem — a detail the Chronicler includes for covenant genealogical purposes. Every king's reign is measured against the Davidic standard. Christ is the fulfillment of that standard: not measured against it but embodying it — the one king whose reign is not bounded by years and whose mother (Mary of Nazareth) is named in the genealogy of the true Davidic heir (Matthew 1:16).
2: Amaziah did what was right in the eyes of YHWH, yet not with a whole heart (lōʾ bᵉlēḇāḇ šālēm). The qualified praise is the Chronicler's most common verdict on the kings: right-but-incomplete. The divided heart is the condition of the covenant people without the new covenant's surgery (Jeremiah 31:33; Ezekiel 36:26). Christ is the antithesis of the divided heart: 'I always do the things that are pleasing to him' (John 8:29); 'my food is to do the will of him who sent me' (John 4:34) — undivided, total, perfect obedience.
3: Once established, Amaziah executed his father's assassins. He waited until 'the kingdom was established for him' — political prudence restraining revenge. The justice he delayed is the Chronicler's occasion to cite the Torah (2 Chronicles 25:4). Christ does not delay justice but fulfills it: 'do you think that I cannot appeal to my Father, and he will at once send me more than twelve legions of angels?' (Matthew 26:53) — the voluntary restraint of the one who had all power.
4: Amaziah did not execute the sons of the assassins, citing Deuteronomy 24:16: 'each person shall die for his own sin.' The individual moral accountability principle drawn from Torah. Christ exhausts this principle on behalf of all: he died for others' sins, not his own (1 Peter 3:18: 'Christ also suffered once for sins, the righteous for the unrighteous') — the one person who could die for others because he had no sin of his own for which to die.
5: The military census: 300,000 troops from Judah and Benjamin, able-bodied men. The enumeration of fighting capacity before battle. Christ does not enumerate his forces — his kingdom does not advance by counting soldiers (Zechariah 4:6: 'not by might, nor by power, but by my Spirit'). The one who could command twelve legions of angels (Matthew 26:53) chose the cross.
6: Amaziah hired 100,000 Israelite mercenaries for 100 talents of silver. The purchase of military help — the covenant equivalent of trusting in an arm of flesh. Christ does not hire help; he sends his Spirit as the 'Helper' (John 14:26, paráklētos) — not a purchased auxiliary but the divine co-worker who comes as gift.
7: The man of God warns against taking the north Israelite troops: 'YHWH is not with Israel — not with all the Ephraimites.' The warning is about contaminated alliances — yoked to those under divine judgment. Paul draws this principle in 2 Corinthians 6:14-15: 'do not be unequally yoked with unbelievers; for what partnership has righteousness with lawlessness?' Christ came to reconcile, not to be contaminated by what he reconciles — 'he was numbered with the transgressors' (Isaiah 53:12) but was not himself a transgressor.
8: 'God has power to help or to cast down' (2 Chronicles 25:8) — the prophet's statement that divine sovereignty underlies all military outcomes. Paul makes the same claim from prison (Philippians 4:13: 'I can do all things through him who strengthens me') and from weakness (2 Corinthians 12:9: 'my power is made perfect in weakness'). The acknowledgment of divine sovereignty is the ground of courage without human alliance.
9: Amaziah's concern about the 100 talents already paid for the mercenaries — the money is lost if they're sent home. The prophet's answer: 'YHWH is able to give you much more than this.' The principle that divine provision exceeds financial loss governs Christ's invitation to the rich young ruler (Matthew 19:21: 'sell what you possess and give to the poor... and come, follow me') — the one who asks for the 100 talents can give 'much more.'
10: Amaziah dismissed the Israelite troops angrily. The separation from contaminated alliances produces conflict — the mercenaries are furious. Following Christ's call also produces conflict: 'I did not come to bring peace, but a sword' (Matthew 10:34). The sword of separation is the cost of undivided allegiance.
11: Amaziah led his troops to the Valley of Salt and struck down 10,000 Edomites. Victory in the designated battle. The covenant reward for the obedient act (sending the mercenaries away) is military success in the appointed theater. Christ's obedience unto death (Philippians 2:8) is followed by the appointed victory: resurrection and exaltation (Philippians 2:9-11).
12: Judah captured 10,000 more Edomites and threw them from the top of the cliff, so that they were all dashed to pieces. A severe act of battle-judgment. The OT battle victory has a violent execution-of-judgment aspect that the NT transforms: Christ 'leads captives in his train' (Ephesians 4:8, citing Psalms 68:18) — the victory still captures enemies but the outcome is now release rather than destruction. 'He has delivered us from the domain of darkness and transferred us to the kingdom of his beloved Son' (Colossians 1:13).
13: The dismissed Israelite mercenaries raided towns of Judah from Samaria to Beth-horon, killing 3,000. The cost of the refused alliance — former allies become attackers. The enemies Christ defeats are not former allies but the powers of sin and death; his victory removes the penalty and power of those forces rather than creating new enemies among the freed.
14: Amaziah brought back the gods of Edom and set them up as his own gods, bowing to them and burning incense to them. The absurdity is complete: he defeated Edom, so he adopts Edom's gods — the gods who demonstrably failed their worshippers. Christ's victory does not include adopting the enemy's religious framework; rather 'he disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame, by triumphing over them in him' (Colossians 2:15). Victory means the conquered powers are exposed, not installed.
15: YHWH's anger burns against Amaziah: 'Why have you sought the gods of a people who did not deliver their people from your hand?' The futility-of-idolatry argument in its sharpest form. Christ makes the same claim about all rival lords: 'all who came before me are thieves and robbers, but the sheep did not listen to them' (John 10:8). The gods who fail their people cannot save others.
16: The prophet is silenced by Amaziah: 'Have we appointed you as a royal counselor? Stop!' The prophet withdraws with the announcement: 'I know that God has determined to destroy you.' The refusal to hear the divine word seals judgment. Christ speaks to the same pattern: 'Whoever is of God hears the words of God. The reason why you do not hear them is that you are not of God' (John 8:47). Rejection of the divine word is the diagnostic of a heart already hardened.
17: Amaziah challenges Joash of Israel: 'Come, let us look at one another in the face.' The provocative challenge after his Edomite victory leads to a disaster. The pride of military success breeding contempt for neighbors — the same pride Uzziah will display in 2 Chronicles 26. Christ warns against this pride-spiral: 'everyone who exalts himself will be humbled' (Luke 14:11).
18: Joash's thistle-and-cedar parable: a thornbush on Lebanon sent to a cedar demanding a marriage alliance; then a wild beast trampled the thornbush. The parable mocks Amaziah's pretension — he is the thistle challenging the cedar. Christ uses parables to expose the same self-inflated pretension (Luke 14:7-11: taking the seats of honor only to be displaced). The kingdom belongs to those who are humble, not those who overreach.
19: 'You have indeed struck down Edom, and your heart has lifted you up in pride.' The diagnosis: military success producing pride. The same sequence appears in Uzziah (2 Chronicles 26:16) and Hezekiah (2 Chronicles 32:25). Christ is the exception — the one in whom success produces no pride, in whom 'I can do nothing on my own' (John 5:30) remains the operative principle even at the apex of his ministry.
20: Amaziah did not listen — 'for it was of God, in order that he might give them into the hand of Joash, because they had sought the gods of Edom.' The divine providential hardening through the fruit of the king's own idolatry. Christ's warning: 'to the one who has, more will be given, and from the one who has not, even what he has will be taken away' (Matthew 13:12) — covenant faithfulness compounds; covenant unfaithfulness also compounds, until the point of handing over.
21: Joash of Israel defeated Judah at Beth-shemesh. The very city whose name means 'house of the sun' becomes the site of Judah's humiliation. The reversal of fortune is complete: the proud challenger is defeated. Christ accepts the apparent defeat of the cross — the humiliation that precedes the glorification — but his defeat was not the fruit of pride; it was the instrument of the victory over death.
22: Judah was defeated before Israel, and every man fled to his tent. The complete rout of the army — covenant curse in military form. The disciples also fled at Christ's arrest: 'they all left him and fled' (Mark 14:50). In both cases the moment of the leader's defeat scatters the followers. But Christ's followers are regathered by resurrection (Matthew 28:10, 16-17), not by the king's military recovery.
23: Joash broke down 400 cubits of Jerusalem's wall — the Ephraim Gate to the Corner Gate. The physical breach of Jerusalem's defenses. The true breach of the enemy's power over Jerusalem comes through Christ: 'on this rock I will build my church, and the gates of hell shall not prevail against it' (Matthew 16:18) — not walls broken but gates that cannot hold against his advance.
24: Joash seized gold, silver, and vessels from the temple, and hostages, and returned to Samaria. The temple stripped by a northern king — a foretaste of the Babylonian stripping to come. Christ's passion includes the stripping of his garments (Mark 15:24) — the reverse despoliation; in his stripping he becomes the true ransom (Mark 10:45).
25: Amaziah lived fifteen years after the death of Joash. The survival after defeat — an extended reign in diminished status. The Chronicler implies that the extra fifteen years are under the shadow of the defeat and apostasy. Christ's resurrection is not diminished survival but total vindication — the opposite of Amaziah's truncated legacy.
26: From the time that Amaziah turned away from YHWH, a conspiracy formed against him in Jerusalem. The apostasy and the political unraveling are linked — one follows the other in the Chronicler's theology. Christ faces conspiracy from the Passion's entry point onward (John 11:53: 'from that day on they made plans to put him to death') — but the conspiracy is the occasion for the fulfillment of God's purpose, not its frustration.
27: Amaziah fled to Lachish but they sent after him and killed him there. Death in flight — the humiliating end of a king who began with partial righteousness and ended in apostasy. Christ also was pursued to his death, but not in flight — 'no one takes it from me, but I lay it down of my own accord' (John 10:18). The contrast between the king fleeing his death and the King who walks toward his is the contrast between involuntary tragedy and voluntary sacrifice.
28: They brought Amaziah on horses and buried him in the City of David. Burial in the royal tomb despite the apostasy — a minimum of covenantal honor preserved. The burial ends the account. Christ was buried 'in a rich man's tomb' (Matthew 27:60), fulfilling Isaiah 53:9, but the tomb was not the end — 'why do you seek the living among the dead?' (Luke 24:5). The burial that concludes every previous king's account is only the midpoint of Christ's story.
